papergraph.title: Multi‐Layer Charge Regulation Engineering for Efficient Enhancement of Aramid Epoxy‐Based Composite at Withstand Voltage Performance
papergraph.citation: Guowei Xia et al. (2026) Multi‐Layer Charge Regulation Engineering for Efficient Enhancement of Aramid Epoxy‐Based Composite at Withstand Voltage Performance doi:10.1002/adfm.76312
papergraph.venue: Advanced Functional Materials
papergraph.abstract: In recent years, fiber‐reinforced epoxy resin‐based composites have become commonly used for insulating components in high‐voltage transmission and transformation equipment. The high withstand voltage performance of materials is crucial to ensuring the safe and stable operation of equipment and the power grid. Therefore, in this paper, a multi‐layer charge regulation engineering was designed on the aramid fiber's surface to regulate the distribution and transmission of charges, thereby achieving an efficient enhancement of the withstand voltage of the composites. This engineering utilizes the charge trapping characteristics of insulating materials and the charge transport properties of semiconductor materials. The effectiveness of this engineering was validated through the tests of flashover voltage and breakdown strength. After constructing the optimal multi‐layer charge regulation structure, the flashover voltage increased by 62.03%, and the breakdown strength improved by 65.22%. Combining charge dissipation and trap distribution, interface bond strength, molecular dynamics simulations, and phase‐field modeling results reveals the mechanism of the multi‐layer charge regulation engineering. This can be summarized as the charge trapping in the F‐Group layer, uniform charge distribution in the ZnO nanowire layer, and deep charge transport in the FePc layer, which together ensure the effective enhancement of the material's withstand voltage performance.

papergraph.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
papergraph.citation: Tongbo He et al. (2025) Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals doi:10.1109/ICEEPS66790.2025.11239566
papergraph.venue: 2025 4th International Conference on Energy and Electrical Power Systems (ICEEPS)
papergraph.abstract: As core equipment for grid condition monitoring, power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, including material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies. This paper proposes an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control. Through retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules, we established a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system. The implementation of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model enables precise simulation of altitude gradient variations. Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s), while significantly reducing testing cycle duration. This research provides technical support for reliability evaluation of power transmission equipment in ultra-high altitude regions such as the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, demonstrating substantial engineering application value.

papergraph.title: Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering
papergraph.citation: Xiaolong Wang et al. (2025) Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering doi:10.1109/ICPICS66386.2025.11347183
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Power, Intelligent Computing and Systems (ICPICS)
papergraph.abstract: In order to improve the flexibility and safety of power supply of electrical engineering systems in scenarios such as high-voltage isolation, complex environments and mobile devices, this paper studies wireless power transmission technology and its application in smart substations, comprehensively and systematically sorts out three types of wireless power supply mechanisms: electromagnetic induction, magnetic resonance coupling and microwave transmission, builds a wireless power supply system framework with magnetic resonance coupling as the core, and deploys the system in 110 kV smart substation scenarios. Test results show that the system achieves an average transmission efficiency of 78.6 % at a coupling distance of 2.8 meters, controls the power fluctuation rate within plus or minus 2.6 %, and reduces the annual interruption frequency to 3 times. The comprehensive operation stability and safety far exceed the traditional wired method. Wireless power transmission shows good engineering practice feasibility and expansion potential, and is suitable for key parts in smart grids that have high requirements for flexibility, safety and efficiency of energy supply.

papergraph.title: Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems
papergraph.citation: Rab Nawaz et al. (2026) Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems doi:10.1038/s41598-026-49195-7
papergraph.venue: Scientific Reports
papergraph.abstract: Modern power systems incorporate ultra-high-voltage transmission lines with series compensation to enhance their reliability and stability. These configurations generate complex data patterns in electrical signals, which present challenges for accurate fault diagnosis. This complexity arises from various potential faults, such as high-impedance faults and inductive-capacitive interactions, compounded by data scarcity owing to privacy and cybersecurity constraints. This study employs a simulation-based analysis to facilitate fault detection, classification, and localization as unified Multi-Classification tasks. The methodology improves the time-frequency domain data through meta-feature engineering and utilizes consensus-based feature selection by combining gradient boosting, variance thresholding, and mutual information. Model learning is conducted using Bayesian optimization within a Stacking Ensemble, integrating Extra Trees, Light Gradient Boosting Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting with an optimized Random Forest meta-classifier. The evaluation considers the accuracy, computational time, inference speed, and memory usage under challenging conditions to address practical limitations. The results indicate that the Stacking Ensemble achieves the highest performance despite its greater computational demand. Extra Trees offers the fastest execution but shows decreased accuracy with increased classification complexity. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine and Extreme Gradient Boosting demonstrate optimized performance across tasks owing to enhanced data processing. These findings confirm the practical applicability of the proposed fault diagnosis framework to modern ultra-high-voltage power systems.

papergraph.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects
papergraph.citation: Yuliang Jiang et al. (2026) Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects doi:10.1109/ICICACS68679.2026.11578464
papergraph.venue: 2026 4th International Conference on Integrated Circuits and Communication Systems (ICICACS)
papergraph.abstract: The cost-effective power transmission is the bulk movement of electrical energy form generating stations to substations located near demand centers. The long distance using high voltage lines and it serves as the critical bridge in the electrical grid, ensuring that energy produced as efficient, remote location reaches homes and industries. The conductors, insulators, towers and transformers contribute major portion of the total investment in power transmission and transformation projects. Selection of suitable main installation materials configurations is crucial for minimizing project costs while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability. The existing optimization algorithms focus on overall project investment and faced the inefficient energy transmission problem, when the existing algorithm are unable to concentrate the multiple crucial aspects simultaneously and stuck into the local optima. To mitigate this problem, the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) is used proposed. The PSO is used to concentrate the multiple aspects simultaneously based on the iterative search spaces utilizing swarm collective experience recursively. The SCT is used to overcome the premature convergence problem which is occurred by the PSO, by forcing the algorithm to resume the exploration when the progress is stopped. The proposed PSO-SCT framework is achieved better results comparing to some models such as Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO), Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO). Thus, the proposed PSO-SCT based approach optimizes conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation selection to reduce main installation materials cost while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.

papergraph.title: Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer
papergraph.citation: Naveen Garg et al. (2025) Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer doi:10.1109/CISES66934.2025.11264962
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent and Secure Engineering Solutions (CISES)
papergraph.abstract: The direct transmission systems using the voltage of one of the largest values, HVDC, that allow transmitting electricity with high efficiency and safety over long distances may be considered one of the most important milestones in the history of electricity engineering and development of the electrical power systems. It has a few strengths such as; it has a lower power loss compared with standard alternating current (AC) systems, is more stable on the grid, and has power density. The fundamental units of the HVDC technology are transformer stations, lines and control systems. HVDC converters technology has made the major focus on voltage source converters (VSCs) and line-commutated converters (LCCs) in power conversion between AC and DC to achieve maximum efficiency of the system. Renewable energy: Here HVDC can adopt renewable energy sources like wind power and solar energy to ease transfer of power through modular and long-distance power transmission of remote generation sites to the centre of consumption. Technology HVDC is being developed in the direction of higher converter efficiency, reduced cost and decreased fault tolerance. Enhanced control algorithms and real-time monitoring software guarantee dynamic stability and easy interface with current grid infrastructures. With the information on the cleaner energy future, an even further progress of the HVDC systems that will be incorporated in the world energy transition on the way to more sustainable and resilient electricity power systems is proceeding.

papergraph.title: Application of Integrated Optical Electric-Field Sensor on the Measurements of Transient Voltages in AC High-Voltage Power Grids
papergraph.citation: S. Xie et al. (2019) Application of Integrated Optical Electric-Field Sensor on the Measurements of Transient Voltages in AC High-Voltage Power Grids doi:10.3390/APP9091951
papergraph.venue: Applied Sciences
papergraph.abstract: Transient voltages in the power grid are the key for the fault analysis of a power grid, optimized insulation design, and the standardization of the high-voltage testing method. The traditional measuring equipment, based on electrical engineering, normally has a limited bandwidth and response speed, which are also featured by a huge size and heavy weight. In this paper, an integrated optical electric-field sensor based on the Pockels effect was developed and applied to measure the transient voltages on the high-voltage conductors in a non-contact measuring mode. The measuring system has a response speed faster than 6 ns and a wide bandwidth ranging from 5 Hz to 100 MHz. Moreover, the sensors have the dimensions of 18 mm by 18 mm by 48 mm and a light weight of dozens of grams. The measuring systems were employed to monitor the lightning transient voltages on a 220 kV overhead transmission line. The switching transient voltages were also measured by the measuring system during the commissioning of the 500 kV middle Tibet power grid. In 2017, 307 lightning transient voltages caused by induction stroke were recorded. The characteristics of these voltage waveforms are different from the standard lightning impulse voltage proposed by IEC standards. Three types of typical switching transient voltage in 500 kV AC power grid were measured, and the peak values of these overvoltages can reach 1.73 times rated voltage.

papergraph.title: Research on system optimization and strategic path of China’s energy security from the perspective of electrical engineering
papergraph.citation: D. Danylchenko et al. (2025) Research on system optimization and strategic path of China’s energy security from the perspective of electrical engineering doi:10.20998/eree.2025.1(10).333342
papergraph.venue: Bulletin of the National Technical University "KhPI". Series: Energy: Reliability and Energy Efficiency
papergraph.abstract: The article presents a comprehensive study of China's energy security from a technical and economic perspective, taking into account current challenges and strategic goals of the energy transition. The main focus is on the quantitative analysis of structural problems that threaten the stability of the energy system. In particular, based on 2023 data, it was found that the structure of energy consumption remains dependent on fossil fuels: the share of coal is 56.4 %, and oil import dependence is 72.6 %. In addition, the probability of overloading the power grid in extreme weather conditions was found to be 7.3 %, indicating the system's vulnerability to climate change. The study modeled the effectiveness of the implementation of Smart Grid technologies, which increased the efficiency of ultra-high voltage power transmission by 18 %. The study also recorded a 40 % increase in the total capacity of virtual power plants and an increase in the internal rate of return of energy storage systems based on lithium batteries to 8.7 %. From the perspective of long-term strategic planning, the proposed multi-energy flow model estimates the cost of transporting 500 km of green hydrogen at 35 yuan/kg, and also forms a standardized power compensation mechanism (0.08 yuan/kW). The empirical results confirm the feasibility of such measures: the rate of rejection of new transmission lines in the northwestern regions has been reduced to 3.2 %, and the introduction of 12 GW of energy storage systems in the Yangtze Delta has reduced peak load by 15%. It is also demonstrated that the cost of hydrogen production from offshore wind energy has reached 0.3 yuan/kWh, or 35 yuan/kg, taking into account market electricity tariffs. The article proposes strategic measures to ensure energy security, including investments in scientific and technological development in the amount of 0.8 % of gross domestic product, modernization of the power grid with funding of 4.2 trillion yuan over the next 10 years, and the creation of an adaptive political monitoring system. These results form the basis for a systematic electrical engineering approach to ensuring China's energy sustainability in the context of global transformations.

papergraph.title: Comparing a Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current and Unified Power Flow Control for Short Circuit Level Reduction in Iraqi 400 Kv Grid
papergraph.citation: A. N. Merzah et al. (2024) Comparing a Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current and Unified Power Flow Control for Short Circuit Level Reduction in Iraqi 400 Kv Grid doi:10.51244/ijrsi.2024.1107018
papergraph.venue: International journal of research and scientific innovation
papergraph.abstract: The demand for electricity has increased significantly in recent years; yet, the transmission and distribution networks are unable to meet unrestrained demands because of resource constraint. Losses in power lines exacerbate the unfavorable conditions for maximum power transfer. Recent decades have seen a rise in the use of FACTS and High Voltage Direct Current transmission in power systems due to its many advantages, which include reduced short circuit level (SCL), enhanced voltage profiles, reduced network power losses, and enhanced system reliability and safety. The paper presents a comparing study of the effectiveness of Unified Power Flow Control (UPFC) and Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current (MT-HVDC) transmission in reducing short circuit current and network power losses for the Iraqi Super High Voltage (ISHV) grid. Modeling of Unified Power Flow Control and Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current transmission is carried out using the Power System Simulator for Engineering (PSS/E) version 32 Package Program. The integration of multi-terminal HVDC transmission to the system resulted in a considerable decrease in short circuit levels and network power losses for the majority of buses in the system, as well as a reduction in the loading on the transmission lines in the southern region of Iraqi Super High Voltage grid, As a result, the South’s network performance will improve, hence improving Iraq’s electrical power system. Furthermore, when compared to installing a UPFC, the results showed that multi-terminal HVDC transmission reduces short circuit levels more efficiently.

papergraph.title: Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale
papergraph.citation: Ping Xiong et al. (2025) Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale doi:10.1109/ICPST65050.2025.11088518
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
papergraph.abstract: The rapid integration of variable renewable energy into county-level power grids has introduced significant operational challenges, particularly transmission congestion and transformer overload caused by bi-directional flow. To overcome the restriction of conventional grid reinforcement solutions focused mainly on infrastructure expansion, the work develops an integrated generation-grid-load-storage coordination framework adapted to scenarios with high penetration of renewables. The solution achieves localized energy balance through optimized energy storage allocation and operational modes control, establishing a resilient power grid architecture capable of grid-connected interaction and autonomous islanded operation. Renovation schemes of building a power system with ultra-high renewable penetration ratios are presented, in which technical routes for the coordination framework are investigated. Performance of key technologies, including grid-forming voltage source converters, smooth mode transition, and the collective control of the coordination system in support of the independent operation and stability of a 100% variable inverter-based renewable energy sources power system, is validated.

papergraph.title: Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan
papergraph.citation: Jiming Song et al. (2023) Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan doi:10.1051/e3sconf/202337002001
papergraph.venue: E3S Web of Conferences
papergraph.abstract: Ultra-high voltage (UHV) power grid is a new transmission and transformation technology, which has the advantages of large transmission capacity, long transmission distance, low line loss, saving land resources and obvious economic benefits. Taking the Zhangbei-xiongan 1000kv AC high voltage power transmission and transformation project as an example, this paper constructs the eco-environmental index system of the UHV transmission and transformation project by using the pressure-state-response model, and constructs a set of 18 indexes eco-environmental index system, it mainly includes three aspects: the eco-environmental pressure index caused by the construction behavior in different construction stages of power transmission and transformation projects, and the eco-environmental state index given by on-the-spot investigation and study of projects, based on the construction unit on the ecological environment impact management response measures given indicators. The index system provides a basis for comprehensive and systematic evaluation of the impact of power transmission and distribution projects on the ecological environment, and finally draws the corresponding project construction evaluation conclusions. In this paper, the eco-environmental impact assessment index system of power transmission and transformation engineering has certain universality, and it has certain reference and guiding significance for the ecological environment evaluation of power transmission and transformation engineering.

papergraph.title: Study on Power Supply Reliability of Sending End Matching Power Access to Ultra-high Voltage AC and DC Hybrid Power Grid
papergraph.citation: Zheng Bowen et al. (2018) Study on Power Supply Reliability of Sending End Matching Power Access to Ultra-high Voltage AC and DC Hybrid Power Grid doi:10.1109/CICED.2018.8592243
papergraph.venue: China International Conference on Electricity Distribution
papergraph.abstract: With the continuous operation of ultra-high voltage (UHV) AC and DC power transmission and transformation projects in Inner Mongolia autonomous region, Inner Mongolia power grid has become a UHV AC and DC hybrid system. However, the development of AC and DC projects is not balanced, and the characteristics of “strong DC and weak AC” are obvious, It is bound to affect the safe and stable operation of UHV power grids, when sending end matching thermal power units connecting to UHV AC and DC hybrid power grid, the reliability of power supply is difficult to be guaranteed. Taking the sending end regional power grid of Xilinhot to Taizhou (Jiangsu Province) UHV DC transmission project (hereinafter referred to as Xitai DC) as the research object, by using Power System Analysis Software Package simulation software, this paper deeply analyzes the influence of high-power matching power supply on the safe and stable operation of the power grid after connecting to the UHV AC and DC hybrid power grid, and put forward the corresponding prevention and control measures.

papergraph.title: Implications of GB/T 22384 Revision for Standardized Testing of Power Grid Security and Stability Control Systems
papergraph.citation: Lei Fu et al. (2025) Implications of GB/T 22384 Revision for Standardized Testing of Power Grid Security and Stability Control Systems doi:10.1109/ICPST65050.2025.11089103
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
papergraph.abstract: The revision of China’s national standard GB/T 22384 addresses critical challenges posed by new-type power grids, characterized by large-scale renewable energy integration, ultra-high voltage AC/DC transmission, and cyber-physical system complexities. Aligned with China’s "dual carbon" strategy and the technical demands of New Power Systems, this revision introduces expanded testing scopes, enhanced network security protection requirements, and refined dynamic simulation methodologies. A case study of the Qing-Yu HVDC project demonstrates the effectiveness of closed-loop test based on real-time digital simulation (RTDS) in validating hierarchical security and stability control systems (SSCS), revealing latent defects such as redundant tripping commands during cascading faults. Innovations lie in integrating RTDS for closed-loop validation of multi-scenario strategies, introducing dynamic state synchronization to mitigate coordination failures, and optimizing lifecycle testing management. The revised standard establishes a robust framework for standardized testing, ensuring reliability and adaptability of SSCS in high-renewable, power electronics-dominated grids. From the main aspects of this revision and the results of engineering empirical tests, the testing methods and technical requirements for SSCS should evolve iteratively in sync with the development process and technological advancements of power systems. SSCS plays a crucial role in ensuring the secure and stable operation of power systems, and the standardization work cannot be achieved just in one effort.

papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Low Carbon and Smart Energy (ICLCSE)
papergraph.abstract: In the context of accelerating urbanisation and the transformation of energy structures, high voltage power cables have emerged as a pivotal transmission method within contemporary power systems. The operational reliability of these entities has been demonstrated to have a direct impact on grid security and power supply quality. Within cable structures, the metallic shielding layer fulfils a dual function: it both suppresses electromagnetic interference and protects communication facilities, while also providing a safe return path for ground faults and preventing electric shock accidents. The present study investigates the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in cable shields. Utilising the electromagnetic transient simulation software PSCAD, a refined cable model incorporating frequency-dependent parameters was constructed. The electrical characteristics and influencing factors of such faults were revealed through analysis of the variation patterns of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions. The present research provides both theoretical and empirical support for the rapid diagnosis and condition assessment in engineering applications.

papergraph.title: Energy consumption analysis of power grid distribution transformers based on an improved genetic algorithm
papergraph.citation: Yubin Lin et al. (2023) Energy consumption analysis of power grid distribution transformers based on an improved genetic algorithm doi:10.7717/peerj-cs.1632
papergraph.venue: PeerJ Computer Science
papergraph.abstract: With the promotion of energy transformation, the utilization ratio of electrical power is progressively rising. Since electrical power is challenging to store, real-time production and consumption become imperative, imposing significant demands on the dependability and operational efficiency of electrical power apparatus. Suppose the load distribution among multiple transformers within a transformer network exhibits inequality. In such instances, it will amplify the total energy consumption during the voltage conversion process, and local, long-term high-load transformer networks become more susceptible to failures. In this article, we scrutinize the matter of transformer energy utilization in the context of electricity transmission within grid systems. We propose a methodology grounded on genetic algorithms to optimize transformer energy usage by dynamically redistributing loads among diverse transformers based on their operational status monitoring. In our experimentation, we employed three distinct approaches to enhance energy efficiency. The experimental findings evince that this approach facilitates swifter attainment of the optimal power level and diminishes the overall energy consumption during transformer operation. Moreover, it exhibits a heightened responsiveness to fluctuations in power demand from the electrical grid. Experimental results manifest that this technique can truncate monitoring time by 27% and curtail the overall energy consumption of the distribution transformer network by 11.81%. Lastly, we deliberate upon the potential applications of genetic algorithms in the realm of power equipment management and energy optimization issues.

papergraph.title: Transformative AI Technologies in High-Voltage Systems: A Review of Advances in Predictive Maintenance, Fault Detection, and Grid Optimization
papergraph.citation: Ian B. Benitez et al. (2025) Transformative AI Technologies in High-Voltage Systems: A Review of Advances in Predictive Maintenance, Fault Detection, and Grid Optimization doi:10.1109/IICAIET67254.2025.11265217
papergraph.venue: International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Engineering and Technology
papergraph.abstract: High voltage engineering has evolved rapidly, driven by the growing need for efficient energy transmission and the integration of renewable energy into modern power grids, including urban areas. Innovations such as HVDC systems are central to this transformation, ensuring that grids can handle the increasing complexity and demand for sustainable energy. However, challenges remain, especially when it comes to coordinating insulation in hybrid AC/DC systems and maintaining the resilience of the overall infrastructure. This review looks at how Artificial Intelligence (AI) can help tackle these challenges, focusing on its role in fault detection, predictive maintenance, and improving system reliability. By comparing traditional methods with AI-driven solutions, we highlight how AI can enhance the scalability, efficiency, and adaptability of power systems. With AI, utilities can predict and prevent faults, optimize grid performance, and seamlessly integrate renewable energy sources into both rural and urban environments. Our goal is to provide insights for researchers, industry professionals, and policymakers on how AI can be harnessed to build more sustainable, resilient, and reliable energy systems. The insights shared here aim to help shape the future of power grids, positioning AI as a key player in the transition to cleaner, more efficient energy solutions.

papergraph.title: INTEGRATIVE APPROACH TO STUDYING INSTRUMENTATION AND ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENTS IN THE CONTEXT OF MODERN POWER INDUSTRY REQUIREMENTS
papergraph.citation: Dariia Vilchynska et al. (2026) INTEGRATIVE APPROACH TO STUDYING INSTRUMENTATION AND ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENTS IN THE CONTEXT OF MODERN POWER INDUSTRY REQUIREMENTS doi:10.70651/3041-248x/2026.1.05
papergraph.venue: Philosophy and Governance
papergraph.abstract: The article investigates and theoretically substantiates the concept of an integrative approach to teaching metrological disciplines for students of electrical engineering specialties. The relevance of the research is driven by the rapid digital transformation of the energy sector (Smart Grid, IoT solutions), which imposes new requirements on engineering competencies. The paper provides a thorough analysis of employer expectations, identifying a critical need for specialists capable of integrating metrological knowledge into remote monitoring processes and automated diagnostics of high-voltage equipment. The author proposes a methodological model that involves the convergence of the content of educational components “Control and Measuring Instruments with the Basics of Metrology” and “Metrology and Electrical Measurements” into a single, cohesive system. This model is based on systemic and process management approaches, allowing for the structuring of educational material from fundamental laws of metrology to their practical application in power engineering. Special attention is paid to the practical tools for implementing integrative learning. The experience of implementing STEM technologies and virtual laboratories, which provide simulation of complex industrial processes without risks to expensive equipment, is revealed. The application of the case method is described in detail using examples of solving real-world energy problems: from analyzing the metrological characteristics of smart meters to minimizing losses in commercial electricity metering systems. The conclusions emphasize that the proposed model not only optimizes learning time but also fosters comprehensive professional thinking in students, essential for solving complex tasks in the modern energy industry.

papergraph.title: Multi-Modal Feature Fusion of Electrical and Infrared Signatures using Advanced Tree-Ensembles for Robust Transmission-Line Fault Detection
papergraph.citation: Dr. R. Mythili et al. (2025) Multi-Modal Feature Fusion of Electrical and Infrared Signatures using Advanced Tree-Ensembles for Robust Transmission-Line Fault Detection doi:10.1109/ICAAIC64647.2025.11330845
papergraph.venue: 2025 4th International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence and Computing (ICAAIC)
papergraph.abstract: Reliable fault detection in high-voltage transmission lines is critical for maintaining power-system stability, yet most current approaches depend on single-source electrical measurements and simulated training data, limiting their robustness under real-world conditions. This work introduces a multi-modal feature-fusion framework that combines synchronized electrical waveforms and infrared (IR) thermographic imagery to overcome those constraints. Field-deployed phasor measurement units (PMUs) capture high-resolution current-voltage signals while co-located thermal cameras provide real-time surface temperature profiles of conductors and insulators. After signal alignment and noise reduction, a dedicated feature-engineering pipeline extracts statistical, spectral, and texture descriptors from both modalities and merges them into a unified representation. Advanced tree-ensemble classifiers—specifically LightGBM and ExtraTrees—are trained to exploit cross-modal correlations for accurate fault detection and type classification. Experiments on a hybrid dataset of laboratory faults and real transmission-corridor measurements demonstrate greater than 99.5 % accuracy and superior resilience to environmental noise compared with electrical-only baselines. The approach eliminates the need for purely synthetic data, supports explainable decision-making through SHAP analysis, and is readily deployable on edge devices for near-instantaneous fault localization and predictive maintenance in modern smart grids Keywords: Multi-modal feature fusion, Transmission-line fault detection, Infrared thermography, Tree-ensemble learning, Smart grid monitoring

papergraph.scenario: transmission-line fault detection
papergraph.benchmark: accuracy, resilience to environmental noise

papergraph.title: MEASUREMENT AND CONTROL METHODS IN ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING
papergraph.citation: Taisiia Serdenko et al. (2025) MEASUREMENT AND CONTROL METHODS IN ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING doi:10.23939/istcmtm2025.02.012
papergraph.venue: Measuring Equipment and Metrology
papergraph.abstract: The article focuses on innovative measurement and control methods in electrical power engineering, specifically addressing challenges of power quality, signal diagnostics, and automation within smart grids. Emphasis is placed on wavelet analysis, smart metering, IoT integration, and automated control systems. These technologies are examined in the context of enhancing the adaptability and efficiency of modern electrical systems in line with Industry 4.0 requirements. Particular emphasis is placed on wavelet analysis, which serves as a universal tool for diagnosing non-stationary electrical signals, assessing power quality, and detecting harmonic distortions. Thanks to its capability for time-frequency localization, wavelet analysis enables effective signal processing and facilitates tasks such as transient process monitoring, voltage flicker analysis, and improving the accuracy of electrical measurements. This methodology opens new prospects for maintaining the stability of energy systems even under the challenging conditions of renewable energy integration. Special attention is given to the analysis of the role of smart technologies in contemporary energy systems. The advantages of Smart Metering systems—which ensure the automatic collection, analysis, and real-time transmission of energy consumption data—are discussed. This enables efficient management of energy resource distribution, reduces energy losses, and enhances transparency in the relationships between consumers and suppliers. The integration of Smart Metering with Internet of Things (IoT) technologies contributes to the creation of adaptive systems capable of responding to changing conditions in real time, thereby ensuring the stability and efficiency of smart grids. The article also explores the prospects of automated control systems that incorporate intelligent data collection devices and adaptive control algorithms. These systems significantly improve monitoring and diagnostics, facilitate the integration of renewable energy sources, and enhance power quality indicators. In particular, the automation of control processes and the implementation of machine learning technologies open new opportunities for forecasting the behavior of energy systems and increasing their resilience. The solutions presented in the study are aimed at creating adaptive, resilient, and high-tech energy systems that meet the modern challenges of Industry 4.0. Through the integration of wavelet analysis, Smart Metering, IoT, and automated control systems, effective management of energy resources, network stability, and the optimization of energy resource usage in the global energy system can be achieved.

papergraph.title: SMART GIC - RESILIENT POWER GRIDS THROUGH GEOPHYSICAL SENSING AND GRAPH-BASED MODELING
papergraph.citation: CHRISTIAN DAVISON DIRISU et al. (2025) SMART GIC - RESILIENT POWER GRIDS THROUGH GEOPHYSICAL SENSING AND GRAPH-BASED MODELING doi:10.70382/tijert.v09i5.010
papergraph.venue: International Journal of Engineering Research and Technology
papergraph.abstract: Geomagnetically induced currents (GICs), which are ground-level quasi-direct currents induced by space-weather phenomena, pose a serious threat to high-voltage power transmission grids, leading to transformer saturation, reactive power loss, and cascading outages during geomagnetic storms. This paper presents a smart, graph-theory-based framework that bridges real-time geophysical sensing with topology-informed grid modeling to protect power networks proactively. We represent the power grid as a graph data structure: substations and transformers are nodes, and transmission lines are edges; we then embed real-time geoelectric field measurements and space-weather forecasts. This feeds into a graph-based GIC algorithm motivated by sparsity principles of sparse-matrix computations and graph neural networks (GNNs), and trained against comparison grids, such as IEEE test systems and China's 500 kV Shandong network. Validation compared performance and accuracy using real storm instances in the UK and Poland. Our method, enhanced with GNN, detects 20% more high-risk grid sections than traditional approaches and enables optimized GIC mitigation hardware placement (e.g., series capacitors, DC-blockers), maintaining computation speed of approximately 18× owing to graph-parallelization. The models also estimate economic trade-offs, enabling cost-efficient investment in grid hardening and operating decisions in real-time during geomagnetic disturbances. This geophysics, electrical engineering, graph theory, and data science-backed cross-disciplinary approach provides an integrated, scalable solution for GIC threat prediction, mitigation, and management. Our findings verify its promise to enhance the resilience of the grid and ensure a secure electric supply in the age of rising space-weather uncertainty.

papergraph.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
papergraph.citation: Hyeongmeen Baik et al. (2025) Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications doi:10.1109/TPEL.2026.3658517
papergraph.venue: arXiv
papergraph.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) power supply topologies, aiming to bridge the gap between DBD applications and power electronics design. Two key aspects are examined: the dependence of the DBD electrical model on reactor geometry, and application-driven requirements for injected waveform characteristics, including shapes, voltage amplitude, frequency, and modulation techniques. On this basis, the paper systematically reviews two major categories of power supplies: sinusoidal types comprising transformerless and transformer-based resonant inverters, and pulsed power supplies (PPSs). The review summarizes performance trade-offs, highlights untested topologies and emerging applications, and offers guidance for advancing high-performance DBD power supply design for next-generation systems.

papergraph.title: Application of Numerical Methods in the Study of the Power Blackout of April 28, 2025, in Spain
papergraph.citation: Javier Bilbao (2025) Application of Numerical Methods in the Study of the Power Blackout of April 28, 2025, in Spain doi:10.37394/232016.2025.20.22
papergraph.venue: WSEAS Transactions on power systems
papergraph.abstract: The power blackout that affected Spain on April 28, 2025, constitutes one of the most significant events in the recent history of the national electricity system. The sudden loss of approximately 15 GW of generation, in a context of high renewable energy penetration and low system inertia, caused an abrupt drop in frequency and the automatic disconnection of the Iberian system from the European electricity system. This study addresses the technical analysis of the event by applying two fundamental numerical methods in electrical engineering: the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method, oriented to the study of transient stability, and the Newton-Raphson method, used for the analysis of steady-state load flows. The combination of both approaches allows a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic and structural behaviour of the electrical grid during the blackout, revealing both the temporal evolution of critical variables and the redistribution of voltages and powers at the affected nodes. The analysis is based on real data, numerical simulations, and a rigorous technical interpretation of the results obtained. The Runge-Kutta method was used to simulate the evolution of system frequency following the loss of generation, considering parameters such as total system inertia, mechanical power, and instantaneous electrical power. The results showed a progressive drop in frequency in the first five seconds of the event, which triggered cascading automatic protections, including load shedding, the disconnection of nuclear power plants, and the separation of the Iberian system from the European one. This dynamic was key to understanding the rapidity with which the instability spread. Meanwhile, the Newton-Raphson method was applied to a ten-node network, allowing significant voltage imbalances to be identified at critical nodes such as N1, N2, and N3. Iterations of the method revealed efficient convergence, and the results showed voltage drops greater than 10% in areas with high renewable penetration and low response capacity. The combined interpretation of both methods showed how the system's low inertia, combined with an uneven distribution of generation and demand, contributed to the power collapse. Furthermore, correlations were observed between frequency drop times and protection activation, as well as between voltage profiles and disconnection points. Based on the results obtained, technical recommendations are made to improve the resilience of the Spanish electricity system. These include increasing inertia through energy storage technologies and synchronous generators, optimizing protection schemes to avoid uncoordinated automatic disconnections, and implementing predictive controllers to manage the high variability of renewable sources. Furthermore, periodic stability simulations are proposed with scenarios of high renewable penetration and critical demand, as well as reinforcing the transmission infrastructure in areas with high load density. Regional energy planning is also suggested, considering not only the balance between generation and demand, but also the structural robustness and operational flexibility of each area. This study demonstrates that the combined application of advanced numerical methods not only allows for the analysis of past events with technical rigor but also constitutes an essential tool for planning, preventing, and mitigating future blackouts in complex and highly interconnected electrical systems.

papergraph.title: System optimization of long-distance energy transportation in China using ultra-high-voltage power transmission
papergraph.citation: Yaru Zhang et al. (2018) System optimization of long-distance energy transportation in China using ultra-high-voltage power transmission doi:10.1063/1.5013177
papergraph.venue: Journal of Renewable and Sustainable Energy
papergraph.abstract: Energy transportation is an important issue for China's electricity system due to the unbalanced distribution of energy sources and electrical demands. The traditional method of energy transportation (e.g., coal transportation) is challenging due to both the long distance of transportation and the large amount of energy that it requires. In recent years, China has been developing ultra-high voltage (UHV) as an alternative way of transmitting energy over a long distance, and many UHV lines are being planned for construction during the 13th Five-Year Plan (FYP) period. This paper develops a system optimization model for exploring the optimal pathway for long-distance energy transportation in China during the 13th FYP period from the perspective of the minimum cost of an energy transportation system. The results show that (a) although UHV transmission will be increasingly adopted in the 13th FYP period, coal transportation will still play an important role; (b) eight UHV lines are suggested to be built and put into use as a priority in the 13th FYP period; (c) the high technology learning effect, electricity demand, and investment costs of power plants would promote the adoption of UHV. Based on these results, some policy suggestions are provided.

papergraph.title: Study on the Safety of Power Frequency Electro-Magnetic Exposure from Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines
papergraph.citation: Ji-kun Guo (2024) Study on the Safety of Power Frequency Electro-Magnetic Exposure from Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines doi:10.1109/APPEEC61255.2024.10922335
papergraph.venue: IEEE PES Asia-Pacific Power and Energy Engineering Conference
papergraph.abstract: To prevent potential electromagnetic hazards associated with electrical equipment, the method of employing electromagnetic exposure limits is a widely accepted technical specification across various countries. This study utilizes a 1:1 human body model positioned under a 550 kV ultra-high voltage transmission line to investigate the effects of electromagnetic exposure at different locations. The results indicate that within the power frequency electromagnetic influence zone of the transmission line, there are instances where exposure levels approach or exceed the 10 kV/m limit. However, due to the shielding effect of the human body's weak conductivity, the maximum induced electric field strength and current density at various body parts from the 550 kV ultra-high voltage transmission line were found to be only 62.665 mV/m and 487.943 11 Alm2, respectively. These values are significantly lower than the significant hazard thresholds of 100 m VIm and 2000 11 Alm2 established in biomedical standards. Moreover, as the distance from the center of the transmission lines increases, electromagnetic exposure decays rapidly. Therefore, despite the issue of power frequency electromagnetic exposure from high-voltage and ultra-high voltage transmission lines approaching or exceeding technical regulation limits, the actual harm to humans remains relatively low.

keynotes.title: Multi‐Layer Charge Regulation Engineering for Efficient Enhancement of Aramid Epoxy‐Based Composite at Withstand Voltage Performance

keynote.title: Multi‐Layer Charge Regulation Engineering for Efficient Enhancement of Aramid Epoxy‐Based Composite at Withstand Voltage Performance
keynote.core_problem: Improving the withstand voltage performance of aramid epoxy-based composites used in high-voltage transmission and transformation equipment.
contributions: Design of a multi-layer charge regulation engineering on the aramid fiber's surface to regulate charge distribution and transmission.
methods: Utilizing charge trapping characteristics of insulating materials and charge transport properties of semiconductor materials.
Constructing an optimal multi-layer charge regulation structure.
experiments_or_evidence: Tests of flashover voltage and breakdown strength.
Validation through increased flashover voltage by 62.03% and improved breakdown strength by 65.22%.

gap_signals.text: Combining charge dissipation and trap distribution, interface bond strength, molecular dynamics simulations, and phase-field modeling results reveals the mechanism of the multi-layer charge regulation engineering.

datasets_or_materials: Aramid fiber
Epoxy resin
F-Group layer
ZnO nanowire layer
FePc layer

important_claims.claim: The multi-layer charge regulation engineering effectively enhances the withstand voltage performance of the composites.
important_claims.evidence: Flashover voltage increased by 62.03%, and breakdown strength improved by 65.22% after constructing the optimal multi-layer charge regulation structure.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a methodological approach for enhancing the electrical insulation properties of composite materials, which can be adapted or extended to other types of composites or applications requiring high withstand voltage performance.

keynotes.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals

keynote.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
keynote.core_problem: Power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, including material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards have limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios.
contributions: Proposed an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control.

Retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules to establish a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system.
methods: Integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules into a test chamber.
Implemented a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model.
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s).
Significantly reduced testing cycle duration.
assumptions: The retrofitting of the test chamber and the implementation of the control model can accurately simulate the complex environmental conditions at ultra-high altitudes.
limitations: The study focuses on the testing methods and does not provide detailed data on the long-term performance or reliability of the sensing terminals under real-world conditions.

gap_signals.text: The study focuses on the testing methods and does not provide detailed data on the long-term performance or reliability of the sensing terminals under real-world conditions.
Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies.

datasets_or_materials: Comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules.
code_or_implementation: Pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm and dynamic gradient control model.

important_claims.claim: The proposed testing methodology effectively replicates multidimensional extreme conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results show that the method can replicate low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s).

important_claims.claim: The testing cycle duration is significantly reduced.
important_claims.evidence: The paper mentions that the new method reduces the testing cycle duration, though specific time reductions are not provided.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The proposed testing methodology and the enhanced test chamber design can be used by researchers and engineers to evaluate and improve the reliability of power transmission and transformation sensing terminals in ultra-high altitude regions, such as the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau.

keynotes.title: Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering

keynote.title: Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering
keynote.core_problem: Improving the flexibility, safety, and efficiency of power supply in electrical engineering systems, especially in challenging environments like high-voltage isolation, complex terrains, and for mobile devices.
contributions: Comprehensive and systematic sorting out of three types of wireless power supply mechanisms: electromagnetic induction, magnetic resonance coupling, and microwave transmission.
Building a wireless power supply system framework with magnetic resonance coupling as the core.
Deployment and testing of the system in a 110 kV smart substation scenario.
methods: Magnetic resonance coupling as the primary mechanism for the wireless power supply system.
experiments_or_evidence: Test results showing an average transmission efficiency of 78.6% at a coupling distance of 2.8 meters.
Controlled power fluctuation rate within ±2.6%.
Reduced annual interruption frequency to 3 times.

gap_signals.text: The paper suggests that wireless power transmission shows good engineering practice feasibility and expansion potential, hinting at further research or development in this area.

important_claims.claim: The wireless power supply system achieves higher operational stability and safety compared to traditional wired methods.
important_claims.evidence: Test results show an average transmission efficiency of 78.6%, controlled power fluctuation rate within ±2.6%, and reduced annual interruption frequency to 3 times.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The detailed analysis and demonstration of the wireless power supply system, particularly its performance metrics and deployment in a real-world scenario, provide a valuable reference for researchers and engineers working on improving energy supply solutions in smart grids and other critical infrastructure.

keynotes.title: Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems

keynote.title: Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems
keynote.core_problem: Accurate fault diagnosis in ultra-high-voltage power systems with series compensation, which is challenging due to complex data patterns, various potential faults, and data scarcity.
contributions: A simulation-based analysis for fault detection, classification, and localization as unified Multi-Classification tasks.
Improvement of time-frequency domain data through meta-feature engineering and consensus-based feature selection.
Model learning using Bayesian optimization within a Stacking Ensemble that integrates multiple machine learning models.
methods: Meta-feature engineering and consensus-based feature selection (gradient boosting, variance thresholding, mutual information).
Bayesian optimization for model learning in a Stacking Ensemble with Extra Trees, Light Gradient Boosting Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting, and an optimized Random Forest meta-classifier.
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluation of the proposed method based on accuracy, computational time, inference speed, and memory usage under challenging conditions.
assumptions: The use of simulation-based analysis can effectively facilitate fault detection, classification, and localization.
limitations: The Stacking Ensemble has a greater computational demand compared to other models.

gap_signals.text: The Stacking Ensemble has a greater computational demand compared to other models.
Data scarcity owing to privacy and cybersecurity constraints.

datasets_or_materials: Simulation-based data for fault detection, classification, and localization.
code_or_implementation: Bayesian-optimized Stacking Ensemble integrating Extra Trees, Light Gradient Boosting Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting with an optimized Random Forest meta-classifier.

important_claims.claim: The Stacking Ensemble achieves the highest performance despite its greater computational demand.
important_claims.evidence: Results from the evaluation indicate that the Stacking Ensemble outperforms other models in terms of accuracy, though it requires more computational resources.

important_claims.claim: Extra Trees offers the fastest execution but shows decreased accuracy with increased classification complexity.
important_claims.evidence: The evaluation results show that while Extra Trees is the fastest, its accuracy drops as the complexity of the classification task increases.

important_claims.claim: Light Gradient Boosting Machine and Extreme Gradient Boosting demonstrate optimized performance across tasks owing to enhanced data processing.
important_claims.evidence: These models show consistent performance across different tasks, attributed to their ability to handle complex data patterns effectively.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The proposed framework and methods can be reused in other fault diagnosis applications in power systems, especially where data is scarce and computational efficiency is a concern. The consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensemble provide a robust approach for handling complex data and improving diagnostic accuracy.

keynotes.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects

keynote.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects
keynote.core_problem: Minimizing the cost of main installation materials (conductors, insulators, towers, transformers) in power transmission and transformation projects while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability. Existing optimization algorithms face issues with inefficient energy transmission and often get stuck in local optima.
contributions: Proposed a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm enhanced with a Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) to address the inefficiency and local optima problems.
methods: Utilized PSO to handle multiple aspects simultaneously through iterative search spaces and swarm collective experience.
Implemented SCT to overcome premature convergence by resuming exploration when progress halts.
experiments_or_evidence: Compared the proposed PSO-SCT framework with Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO) and Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO).

gap_signals.text: Existing algorithms struggle with optimizing multiple crucial aspects simultaneously and often get stuck in local optima, leading to inefficient energy transmission.

important_claims.claim: The proposed PSO-SCT based approach optimizes conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation selection to reduce main installation materials cost while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.
important_claims.evidence: The PSO-SCT framework achieved better results compared to SPSO and FIPSO.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The PSO-SCT framework can be applied to other multi-objective optimization problems in engineering and beyond, where avoiding local optima and ensuring efficient exploration are critical.

keynotes.title: Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer

keynote.title: Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer
keynote.core_problem: Efficient and safe long-distance power transmission with lower power loss, higher stability, and better power density compared to AC systems.
contributions: Focus on the development of HVDC technology, particularly voltage source converters (VSCs) and line-commutated converters (LCCs).
Integration of renewable energy sources such as wind and solar into the power grid using HVDC for efficient transfer.
Improvement in converter efficiency, cost reduction, and fault tolerance.
methods: Utilization of VSCs and LCCs for power conversion between AC and DC.
Enhancement of control algorithms and real-time monitoring software for dynamic stability and easy integration with existing grid infrastructures.
assumptions: Renewable energy sources like wind and solar are key to a cleaner energy future.
The need for more sustainable and resilient electricity power systems is driving the further development of HVDC systems.

gap_signals.text: Further progress in HVDC systems is needed to support the world's energy transition towards more sustainable and resilient power systems.

important_claims.claim: HVDC has lower power loss, higher stability, and better power density compared to AC systems.
important_claims.evidence: The text mentions that HVDC allows transmitting electricity with high efficiency and safety over long distances, which is a key advantage over standard AC systems.

important_claims.claim: HVDC can efficiently integrate renewable energy sources like wind and solar into the power grid.
important_claims.evidence: The paper states that HVDC can adopt renewable energy sources to ease the transfer of power through modular and long-distance power transmission from remote generation sites to the center of consumption.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The focus on HVDC technology, especially VSCs and LCCs, and its role in integrating renewable energy sources provides a foundation for further research into improving power transmission efficiency and sustainability.

keynotes.title: Application of Integrated Optical Electric-Field Sensor on the Measurements of Transient Voltages in AC High-Voltage Power Grids

keynote.title: Application of Integrated Optical Electric-Field Sensor on the Measurements of Transient Voltages in AC High-Voltage Power Grids
keynote.core_problem: Traditional measuring equipment for transient voltages in high-voltage power grids has limited bandwidth, slow response speed, and is bulky and heavy. There is a need for more efficient and compact solutions.
contributions: Development of an integrated optical electric-field sensor based on the Pockels effect for non-contact measurement of transient voltages in high-voltage conductors.
The new sensor offers faster response times (faster than 6 ns) and a wider bandwidth (5 Hz to 100 MHz).
The sensor is significantly smaller and lighter compared to traditional equipment, with dimensions of 18 mm by 18 mm by 48 mm and a weight of dozens of grams.
methods: Utilization of the Pockels effect for the design of the optical electric-field sensor.
Non-contact measurement of transient voltages in high-voltage conductors.
experiments_or_evidence: Measurement of lightning transient voltages on a 220 kV overhead transmission line, recording 307 induction stroke events in 2017.
Measurement of switching transient voltages during the commissioning of the 500 kV middle Tibet power grid, where peak values of overvoltages reached 1.73 times the rated voltage.
assumptions: The Pockels effect can be effectively utilized for the development of a compact and fast-response sensor.
limitations: The paper does not discuss potential limitations or challenges in the deployment and maintenance of the sensor in various environmental conditions.

gap_signals.text: The paper does not discuss potential limitations or challenges in the deployment and maintenance of the sensor in various environmental conditions.
Further research could explore the long-term reliability and performance of the sensor under different environmental conditions.
Future work might focus on integrating additional functionalities into the sensor, such as real-time data processing and wireless communication capabilities.

keynotes.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals

keynote.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
keynote.core_problem: Power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, such as material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards are limited in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios.
contributions: Proposed an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control.

Retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules to establish a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system.
Implemented a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model for precise simulation of altitude gradient variations.
methods: Multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control.
Integration of ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules into a comprehensive test chamber.
Use of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm and a dynamic gradient control model.
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s).
Significant reduction in testing cycle duration.

gap_signals.text: Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies.

datasets_or_materials: Comprehensive test chamber retrofitted with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules.
code_or_implementation: Pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm and dynamic gradient control model.

keynotes.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects

keynote.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects
keynote.core_problem: Optimizing the cost of main installation materials (conductors, insulators, towers, transformers) in power transmission and transformation projects while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability, and avoiding local optima in the optimization process.
contributions: Introduction of a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm enhanced with a Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) to address the limitations of existing optimization algorithms.
methods: Utilization of PSO to handle multiple aspects of the project simultaneously through iterative search spaces.
Incorporation of SCT to prevent premature convergence and ensure continuous exploration when progress stalls.
experiments_or_evidence: Comparison of the proposed PSO-SCT framework with Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO) and Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO).

gap_signals.text: Existing optimization algorithms are inefficient in handling multiple critical aspects and often get stuck in local optima.

datasets_or_materials: Main installation materials: conductors, insulators, towers, and transformers.

important_claims.claim: The PSO-SCT based approach optimizes the selection of conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation to reduce costs while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.
important_claims.evidence: The proposed PSO-SCT framework achieved better results compared to SPSO and FIPSO models.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work can be used as a foundation for further research into more efficient and effective optimization techniques for large-scale infrastructure projects, particularly in the energy sector. The use of PSO with SCT provides a robust method to avoid local optima, which is a common issue in complex optimization problems.

keynotes.title: Study on the Safety of Power Frequency Electro-Magnetic Exposure from Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines

keynote.title: Study on the Safety of Power Frequency Electro-Magnetic Exposure from Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines
keynote.core_problem: To investigate and assess the safety of power frequency electromagnetic exposure from ultra-high voltage (550 kV) transmission lines, particularly focusing on the potential hazards to human health.
contributions: Provided a detailed analysis of the electromagnetic exposure levels under a 550 kV ultra-high voltage transmission line using a 1:1 human body model.
Demonstrated that the induced electric field strength and current density in the human body are significantly lower than the biomedical standards for significant hazard.
methods: Used a 1:1 human body model positioned under a 550 kV ultra-high voltage transmission line.
Measured the electromagnetic exposure at different locations within the power frequency electromagnetic influence zone.
experiments_or_evidence: Conducted measurements of the induced electric field strength and current density in the human body model.
Observed that the maximum induced electric field strength and current density were 62.665 mV/m and 487.943 μA/m², respectively, which are below the significant hazard thresholds.
assumptions: The human body's weak conductivity provides a shielding effect against electromagnetic exposure.
limitations: The study is based on a 1:1 human body model, which may not fully replicate the variability in human anatomy and physiology.
The study focuses on a specific voltage level (550 kV), and results may not be generalizable to other voltage levels.

gap_signals.text: The study focuses on a specific voltage level (550 kV), and results may not be generalizable to other voltage levels.
The study is based on a 1:1 human body model, which may not fully replicate the variability in human anatomy and physiology.
There is a need for more comprehensive studies involving different voltage levels and real human subjects to better understand the full range of electromagnetic exposure effects.

important_claims.claim: Despite some instances where exposure levels approach or exceed the 10 kV/m limit, the actual harm to humans remains relatively low due to the shielding effect of the human body.
important_claims.evidence: The maximum induced electric field strength and current density in the human body model were 62.665 mV/m and 487.943 μA/m², respectively, which are significantly lower than the significant hazard thresholds of 100 mV/m and 2000 μA/m².

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides valuable data and insights into the safety of power frequency electromagnetic exposure from ultra-high voltage transmission lines, which can be used as a reference for future research and policy-making in the field of electromagnetic safety.

Application of the sensor in a non-contact mode to measure voltages on high-voltage conductors.
experiments_or_evidence: Measurement of lightning transient voltages on a 220 kV overhead transmission line, recording 307 events in 2017.
Monitoring of switching transient voltages during the commissioning of the 500 kV middle Tibet power grid, observing peak values up to 1.73 times the rated voltage.
assumptions: The Pockels effect can be effectively utilized for developing an optical sensor capable of measuring transient voltages in high-voltage power grids.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles
a fault diagnosis framework using consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles, achieving high performance in fault detection, classification, and localization
the Stacking Ensemble has a greater computational demand compared to other models
Full text excerpt:

The methodology improves the time-frequency domain data through meta-feature engineering and utilizes consensus-based feature selection by combining gradient boosting, variance thresholding, and mutual information.
The evaluation considers the accuracy, computational time, inference speed, and memory usage under challenging conditions to address practical limitations.
The results indicate that the Stacking Ensemble achieves the highest performance despite its greater computational demand.
Extra Trees offers the fastest execution but shows decreased accuracy with increased classification complexity.
These findings confirm the practical applicability of the proposed fault diagnosis framework to modern ultra-high-voltage power systems.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: power transmission and transformation projects

references: Yuliang Jiang et al. (2026) Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects doi:10.1109/ICICACS68679.2026.11578464

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique
optimization of main installation materials cost while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability
The long distance using high voltage lines and it serves as the critical bridge in the electrical grid, ensuring that energy produced as efficient, remote location reaches homes and industries.
Full text excerpt:

The existing optimization algorithms focus on overall project investment and faced the inefficient energy transmission problem, when the existing algorithm are unable to concentrate the multiple crucial aspects simultaneously and stuck into the local optima.
To mitigate this problem, the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) is used proposed.
The SCT is used to overcome the premature convergence problem which is occurred by the PSO, by forcing the algorithm to resume the exploration when the progress is stopped.
The proposed PSO-SCT framework is achieved better results comparing to some models such as Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO), Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO).

Thus, the proposed PSO-SCT based approach optimizes conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation selection to reduce main installation materials cost while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: genetic algorithm
A methodology using genetic algorithms to optimize transformer energy usage by dynamically redistributing loads, leading to reduced energy consumption and monitoring time.
In this article, we scrutinize the matter of transformer energy utilization in the context of electricity transmission within grid systems.
Full text excerpt:

We propose a methodology grounded on genetic algorithms to optimize transformer energy usage by dynamically redistributing loads among diverse transformers based on their operational status monitoring.
In our experimentation, we employed three distinct approaches to enhance energy efficiency.
The experimental findings evince that this approach facilitates swifter attainment of the optimal power level and diminishes the overall energy consumption during transformer operation.
Experimental results manifest that this technique can truncate monitoring time by 27% and curtail the overall energy consumption of the distribution transformer network by 11.81%.
Lastly, we deliberate upon the potential applications of genetic algorithms in the realm of power equipment management and energy optimization issues.

Reliable fault detection in high-voltage transmission lines is critical for maintaining power-system stability, yet most current approaches depend on single-source electrical measurements and simulated training data, limiting their robustness under real-world conditions.
This work introduces a multi-modal feature-fusion framework that combines synchronized electrical waveforms and infrared (IR) thermographic imagery to overcome those constraints.

Thanks to its capability for time-frequency localization, wavelet analysis enables effective signal processing and facilitates tasks such as transient process monitoring, voltage flicker analysis, and improving the accuracy of electrical measurements.

Full text excerpt:
This paper presents a smart, graph-theory-based framework that bridges real-time geophysical sensing with topology-informed grid modeling to protect power networks proactively.

This feeds into a graph-based GIC algorithm motivated by sparsity principles of sparse-matrix computations and graph neural networks (GNNs), and trained against comparison grids, such as IEEE test systems and China's 500 kV Shandong network.
Validation compared performance and accuracy using real storm instances in the UK and Poland.

Our method, enhanced with GNN, detects 20% more high-risk grid sections than traditional approaches and enables optimized GIC mitigation hardware placement (e.g., series capacitors, DC-blockers), maintaining computation speed of approximately 18× owing to graph-parallelization.

This geophysics, electrical engineering, graph theory, and data science-backed cross-disciplinary approach provides an integrated, scalable solution for GIC threat prediction, mitigation, and management.

references: Javier Bilbao (2025) Application of Numerical Methods in the Study of the Power Blackout of April 28, 2025, in Spain doi:10.37394/232016.2025.20.22

evidence_type_annotations.claim: fourth-order Runge-Kutta method | Newton-Raphson method
technical analysis of the power blackout using numerical methods to understand the dynamic and structural behavior of the electrical grid, leading to recommendations for improving system resilience
Furthermore, correlations were observed between frequency drop times and protection activation, as well as between voltage profiles and disconnection points.
Full text excerpt:

This study addresses the technical analysis of the event by applying two fundamental numerical methods in electrical engineering: the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method, oriented to the study of transient stability, and the Newton-Raphson method, used for the analysis of steady-state load flows.

The combination of both approaches allows a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic and structural behaviour of the electrical grid during the blackout, revealing both the temporal evolution of critical variables and the redistribution of voltages and powers at the affected nodes.
The analysis is based on real data, numerical simulations, and a rigorous technical interpretation of the results obtained.

The Runge-Kutta method was used to simulate the evolution of system frequency following the loss of generation, considering parameters such as total system inertia, mechanical power, and instantaneous electrical power.

The results showed a progressive drop in frequency in the first five seconds of the event, which triggered cascading automatic protections, including load shedding, the disconnection of nuclear power plants, and the separation of the Iberian system from the European one.
Meanwhile, the Newton-Raphson method was applied to a ten-node network, allowing significant voltage imbalances to be identified at critical nodes such as N1, N2, and N3.
Iterations of the method revealed efficient convergence, and the results showed voltage drops greater than 10% in areas with high renewable penetration and low response capacity.
The combined interpretation of both methods showed how the system's low inertia, combined with an uneven distribution of generation and demand, contributed to the power collapse.
